channel.title: The Economist - World in Brief
channel.description: Catch up quickly on the global stories that matter

item.title: World in Brief - March 12, 2026
item.description:
Oil prices rose despite the International Energy Agency announcing it would release an unprecedented 400m barrels of oil from its reserves. Brent crude, the global oil benchmark, jumped back past $100 a barrel during early trading in Asia. The blockade of the Strait of Hormuz has curtailed global oil supply by roughly 20m barrels a day.

The Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, the Iranian regime’s security force, warned countries to “get ready for oil to be $200 a barrel”. Iran struck two oil tankers in the Persian Gulf, setting them ablaze and killing at least one crew member. Iran’s president said America and Israel could only end the war if they guarantee against future aggression. Meanwhile Israel launched an “expansive wave of strikes” on Beirut, Lebanon’s capital.

The Pentagon reportedly told Congress that the first six days of the war in Iran cost America more than $11bn. It also said it was investigating a strike on a girls’ school in Iran, which killed 175 people. According to the New York Times, a preliminary investigation found that the building was targeted as a result of outdated American intelligence data. President Donald Trump had previously blamed Iran.

America launched investigations into several of its trading partners, including the EU, China, Japan and India. The probes will examine their alleged “excess capacity and production in manufacturing sectors”, but are seen as a precursor for the Trump administration to raise new duties on imports. America’s Supreme Court struck down many of Mr Trump’s tariffs last month, but he vowed to reimpose them.

Britain’s government released hundreds of files related to Peter Mandelson, its former ambassador to America who was recalled over his links with Jeffrey Epstein, a dead sex offender. The files include warnings about the appointment’s “reputational risk”. A senior adviser is quoted as having called the process “weirdly rushed”. The revelations raise further questions about the judgment of Sir Keir Starmer, threatening his premiership.

Atlassian said that it would axe 1,600 staff, about 10% of its global workforce. Mike Cannon-Brookes, the Australian software firm’s boss, said that artificial intelligence was changing the type of skills that were needed. In common with many other software-makers, Atlassian’s share price has plummeted as it reckons with AI models that are adept at coding, threatening its business.

As global drugmakers scrabble for a share of India’s fast-growing weight-loss market, the country’s drug regulator cautioned companies against “misleading” advertisements. In India weight-loss drugs are meant to be prescribed by a pharmacist and the country does not allow prescription products to be advertised. The regulator warned that even a promotion “under the pretext of disease awareness”, would be considered a “surrogate advertisement”.

Follow our coverage of the war in Iran.

How to ease an energy shock

Expect more turbulence in markets on Thursday, as the world reacts to Iran’s “most intense” operation of the war so far. The previous day Iran’s regime vowed to “not allow even a single litre of oil” through the Strait of Hormuz, and said it regarded any vessel connected to America, Israel or its partners as a legitimate target. Projectiles struck three ships in or near the waterway; two tankers in the Persian Gulf were set ablaze. Shell, the world’s largest LNG trader, declared force majeure on several contracts, excusing the company from meeting its obligations.

Governments are scrambling to control prices. On Wednesday the 32, mostly Western, members of the International Energy Agency announced that they would collectively release 400m barrels of oil from reserves, roughly equivalent to four days’ worth of global consumption.

Meanwhile Donald Trump’s promise that American warships would escort tankers through the strait “if needed” looks far-fetched. The US Navy has repeatedly denied shipowners’ requests in recent days, according to Reuters.

American hopes for a Kurdish uprising dashed

The peshmerga, the military forces of Iraq’s Kurdistan Region whose name translates to “those who face death”, were poised to cross into Iran following America’s and Israel’s attacks. Now they are hesitating. Is it a ruse? The swift collapse of central authority in Iran, which America and Israel expected after killing Ali Khamenei and wrecking much of Iran’s military, has not happened. Iran has aimed missiles and drones at the Kurdish region and warned their leaders against backing an insurgency. Turkey and Iraq have also rattled sabres, wary that Kurdish gains might embolden those dreaming of a Kurdish state.

Donald Trump has accepted the new reality. “I don’t want the Kurds going in,” he said—for their safety, of course. Israel, which tried to ignite an uprising, is probing other fronts. The loyalties of Iran’s ethnic minorities are divided. Many are Shia and fear domination by more numerous Sunni neighbours and the foreigners waging war. But in the past Iran has unravelled when its regimes collapsed.

Sign up to enjoy a mind-expanding mix of stories, delivered six days a week

America is racing China to get back to the Moon

On Thursday Jared Isaacman, the head of America’s space agency, will outline what’s next for its Artemis II mission—set to send astronauts round the Moon for the first time in 54 years. Originally scheduled for early February, lift-off has been repeatedly delayed by problems with the rocket’s plumbing.

While Artemis II has been having various leaks plugged NASA has been busy re-engineering the entire Artemis programme. This reflects the growing realisation that China’s less-ambitious programme might put its first astronauts on the Moon before America gets back there. America’s previous lofty goal of landing Artemis III on the Moon next year has been replaced by a slower but steadier approach. NASA will first test out lunar landers developed by Elon Musk’s SpaceX and Jeff Bezos’s Blue Origin, and has deferred its planned Moon landing until 2028. In December Mr Isaacman warned Congress that if China pulls ahead, America “may never catch up”.

A Medicaid funding fight in Minnesota 

In February the Trump administration launched a “War on Fraud”. A battle is playing out in a Minnesota courtroom. On Thursday parties will gather for a hearing over the federal government’s withholding of a quarter of a billion in funding for Medicaid —government health care for the poor—for the Democrat-run state.

The administration is linking the funding freeze to a sprawling welfare scandal thought to have cost Minnesota taxpayers hundreds of millions. Prosecutors have been bringing fraud-related charges since before Donald Trump’s second term, and have indicted almost 100 people. Now the Trump administration is pulling new levers—and more pain could be coming. If “Minnesota fails to clean up the systems”, warned Mehmet Oz, the federal director of Medicaid, it will “rack up a billion dollars” in withheld payments this year. The state argues it is trying to address fraud and that the government is “weaponising” health-care funding as political punishment.

The grand old man of British art

At 88 years old, British painter David Hockney remains innovative and inquisitive. “A Year In Normandie And Some Other Thoughts About Painting” opened on Thursday at London’s Serpentine North gallery. Inspired by the Bayeux Tapestry, a Norman artefact, its title piece is a continuous frieze that wraps around the gallery interior.

There the resemblance to the 70m-long embroidered cloth ends. Mr Hockney’s work is no battle scene, but a pastoral panorama assembled from numerous digital paintings created on his iPad during the lockdowns of 2020. Its stillness recalls a second source: 14th-century scroll paintings from China.

To pass along it is to accompany the artist on a slow and deeply peaceful walk through the seasons. There are also ten new pictures in the gallery’s centre—five portraits, five semi-abstractions, meditating on similar natural themes, and looking at age-old themes through a sharp modern lens.

Daily Quiz

We will serve you a new question each weekday. On Friday your challenge is to give us all five answers and tell us the theme. Email your responses (and your home city and country) by 1700 GMT on Friday to QuizEspresso@economist.com. We’ll pick three winners at random and crown them on Saturday.

Thursday: What is another term for someone who works as a stable hand?

Wednesday: What was the biggest hit by DNCE, a rock band whose lead singer was Joe Jonas?

All of life is a foreign country.

Jack Kerouac
